coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic fuel cell with N-CQDs electron warehouses mediated ZnIn2S4/tubular g-C3N4/Ti 2D/0D/2D photoanode for efficient organic pollutant degradation and electricity generation

coredata.dc:creator: Han, Zhuofan
Zhong, Dengjie
Xu, Yunlan
Chang, Haixing
Dong, Lin
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)/ZnIn2S4/tubular g-C3N4(TCN)/Ti photoanode was successfully prepared by a simple one-pot solvothermal method and silica sol drop coating method, which was assembled with CuxO/Cu cathode to construct photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) to degrade organic pollutants and generate electricity. The photoanode material aims to promote the separation and uniform transfer of photogenerated electron through the synergistic effect of Schottky junction and S-type heterojunction of 0D quantum dots. The PFC with N-CQDs-doped photoanode possessed strong power generation capacity (71.10·μW-cm−2) and high photoelectric conversion efficiency (0.098 %), which were 1.72 and 1.94 times that of the PFC with ZnIn2S4/tubular g-C3N4/Ti photoanode. It degraded more than 95 % of rhodamine B, 83.44 % of tetracycline and 50.66 % of bisphenol A within 30 min. The excellent performance of the developed PFC is attributed to the synergistic effect of different heterojunction in photoanode, and the improvement of absorption and utilization of visible light by doping N-CQDs. Both free radicals (•OH, •O2 −) and non-free radicals (h+, 1O2) were involved in the degradation process of pollutants. It is noteworthy that, due to the Schottky junction, the N-CQDs could act as electron warehouses to promote the production of H2O2, which could enhance the degradation effect. We believe that this research provides a new direction for the development and research work on efficient photoanodes.
Synopsis N-CQDs was introduced into PFC photoanode to construct the synergistic effect of Schottky junction and S heterojunction to simultaneously realize efficient pollutants degradation and power generation.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic fuel cell with N-CQDs electron warehouses mediated ZnIn2S4/tubular g-C3N4/Ti 2D/0D/2D photoanode for efficient organic pollutant degradation and electricity generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Technology, Chongqing 400054, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Technology, Chongqing 400054, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)/ZnIn2S4/tubular g-C3N4(TCN)/Ti photoanode was successfully prepared by a simple one-pot solvothermal method and silica sol drop coating method, which was assembled with CuxO/Cu cathode to construct photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) to degrade organic pollutants and generate electricity. The photoanode material aims to promote the separation and uniform transfer of photogenerated electron through the synergistic effect of Schottky junction and S-type heterojunction of 0D quantum dots. The PFC with N-CQDs-doped photoanode possessed strong power generation capacity (71.10·μW-cm−2) and high photoelectric conversion efficiency (0.098 %), which were 1.72 and 1.94 times that of the PFC with ZnIn2S4/tubular g-C3N4/Ti photoanode. It degraded more than 95 % of rhodamine B, 83.44 % of tetracycline and 50.66 % of bisphenol A within 30 min. The excellent performance of the developed PFC is attributed to the synergistic effect of different heterojunction in photoanode, and the improvement of absorption and utilization of visible light by doping N-CQDs. Both free radicals (•OH, •O2 −) and non-free radicals (h+, 1O2) were involved in the degradation process of pollutants. It is noteworthy that, due to the Schottky junction, the N-CQDs could act as electron warehouses to promote the production of H2O2, which could enhance the degradation effect. We believe that this research provides a new direction for the development and research work on efficient photoanodes.

N-CQDs was introduced into PFC photoanode to construct the synergistic effect of Schottky junction and S heterojunction to simultaneously realize efficient pollutants degradation and power generation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy crisis and environmental pollution are two major problems facing mankind in the 21st century [1]. Photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) can degrade organic pollutants and produce electricity simultaneously, so it is expected to solve both the problems at the same time [2]. Photoanode of PFC generates photogenerated electrons and holes under the excitation of light, both of which can directly or indirectly participate in the degradation of organic pollutants on the surface of photoanode, and some photogenerated electrons are transferred to cathode through external circuit to form current, and participate in the reaction on the surface of cathode. Therefore, the properties of photoanode have a significant influence on PFC performance [3]. At present, the bare photoanode catalyst materials studied, including TiO2 [4–6], WO3 [7], g-C3N4 [8,9], MoS2 [10], α-Fe2O3 [11] etc., have some problems, such as wide band gap, high recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes, low electron transfer efficiency, resulting in low light utilization rate, high impedance and low photoelectric conversion efficiency [12]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop new and more efficient photoanode materials to solve these problems.

Among photocatalyst materials, hollow tube g-C3N4 (TCN) has attracted much attention because of its large specific surface area and unique electron transport mode [13]. Its distinct hollow tube construction can cause the incident light to reflect/refract on the inner wall several times, thus improving the light capture ability [14]. TCN can not only transfer electrons directly and rapidly in a specific direction because of its radial carrier transport characteristics, so as to realize space charge separation of carriers, but also provide rich active sites because of its large specific surface area, thus improving photocatalytic efficiency [15]. Unfortunately, the single TCN material still has the problems of low visible light utilization rate and high photogenerated electron-hole recombination rate [14]. The solutions include ion doping [16], building heterojunction [10], etc. Among them, building heterojunction is an excellent method, which may create additional electron transfer channel to increase the separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improve the utilization rate of visible light.

Among many semiconductors compounded with TCN, ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) has strong visible light adsorption capacity because of its narrow bandgap and high electron transfer efficiency due to its layered structure [17]. Combining TCN with ZIS can form 2D/2D S-type heterojunction. Unlike conventional Z-scheme and II-type heterojunctions, due to its unique built-in electric field, the S-type heterojunction can not only effectively separate photogenerated carriers, but also retain the high redox capabilities of the two semiconductors. However, ZnIn2S4, as a flexible material, its ultrathin ZnIn2S4 nanosheets are prone to wrinkling during the assembly process [18–20], resulting in the failure to realize intimate contact between interfaces of TCN with ZIS at the nanometer level. This leads to inhomogeneous electron transfer, which greatly reduces the effectiveness of TCN/ZIS S-type heterojunction.

N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), as “zero-dimensional” carbon-based nanomaterials, are considered an excellent candidate for interfacial modulation because of its excellent electron transfer capability and good biocompatibility [21]. Introducing N-CQDs into the interface of TCN/ZIS S-type heterojunction can not only realize the close contact of TCN and ZIS, but also greatly enhance the absorption and utilization of visible light and even ultraviolet light by the composite material because of the inherent upconversion photoluminescence characteristics of N-CQDs. Moreover, compared with quantum dots (CQDs), the N atoms in N-CQDs have a strong electron affinity [22], so they play a better role in promoting the transfer of electrons. Because N-CQDs have large work function, when they are in contact with TCN and ZIS, Schottky junctions (N-CQDs/TCN and N-CQDs/ZIS) can be formed on the interface. Thus, electrons will spontaneously move from TCN and ZIS towards N-CQDs until the work functions on both sides of the contact are equal. However, in the absence of light excitation, the ability of semiconductor to provide electrons is very limited [23]. Under the irradiation of light, S-type semiconductor heterojunction can provide photogenerated electrons, while 0D/2D Schottky junctions (N-CQDs/TCN and N-CQDs/ZIS) will constantly plunder photogenerated electrons from S-type semiconductor heterojunction, forming an “electron storehouse”, which greatly facilitates the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. In summary, N-CQDs can not only act as an electron transfer medium between ZIS and TCN through 0D/2D N-CQDs/ZIS and N-CQDs/TCN Schottky junctions, but also make the electron transfer more uniform and reduce the electrode internal resistance by enhancing the contact, thus giving full play to the advantages of S-type heterojunction and Schottky junction. Unfortunately, there are few studies on improving the performance of photoelectrode in PFC system by doping QDs.

According to the analysis just done, N-CQDs mediated ZnIn2S4 combined tubal g-C3N4 composite (N-CQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti) photoanode was prepared using a facile one-pot solvothermal method and silica sol drop-coating method. In the prepared photoanode, TCN and ZIS form S-type heterojunction, and N-CQDs form Schottky junction with TCN and ZIS respectively. TCN/ZIS S-type heterojunction generates photogenerated electrons under illumination, and retains the good redox ability of photoelectrode materials. As an electron transport medium, Schottky junction greatly improves the carrier separation ability by capturing photogenerated electrons generated by TCN/ZIS S-type heterojunction. That is to say, in N-CQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanode, through the perfect combination of S-type heterojunction and Schottky junction, its synergistic effect will produce an effect of 1 + 1 > 2. A photocatalytic fuel cell was assembled with N-CQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanode and CuxO/Cu cathode to degrade rhodamine B (RhB), tetracycline (TC) and bisphenol A (BPA) and generate electricity. Because of widespread use and extensive research of RhB, TC and BPA in the fields of dyes, pharmaceuticals and chemicals, respectively, they were chosen as degradation targets, which facilitates the comparison of research results [24–26]. The electricity generation and degradation performance, stability, visible-light response, and main active substance (ROS) of N-CQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu PFC were explored. The mechanism of the pollutant degradation and power production of the constructed PFC was also examined.

Tetraethyl silicate (TEOS, 98 %), bisphenol A (BPA) and tetracycline (TC, 99 %) were from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. P-benzoquinone (p-BQ, 97 %), ethanol (99.7 %), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA, 99 %), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 99 %) and furfuryl alcohol (FA, 98 %) were obtained from Chongqing Chuan dong Chemical Co., Ltd., China., Ltd. Urea (99 %), Melamine, dicalcium phosphate (KCl, 99.5 %), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, 99.8 %), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99 %), RhB (99 %), citric acid (≥99.5 %) and Thioacetamide (TAA, ≥99 %) were obtained from Chengdu Cologne Chemicals Co., Ltd., China. Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2), potassium titanium oxalate (C9K2O9Ti), indium chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O, ≥99 %) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., China. The reagents mentioned above are all of analytical grade. Acetonitrile (≥99.99), oxalic acid (≥99.99), methanol (≥99.9) and acetic acid (≥99.9) used for HPLC analysis were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., China, all of which were of chromatographic purity. The solutions have all been prepared using deionized water. Ti and Cu sheets of the same size (5 cm × 3 cm × 1 mm) were bought from Hongtai Metal Products Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and used after they were polished with sandpaper, ultrasonically cleaned with deionized water, ethanol and acetone, respectively, for 30 min.

According to the previous research [13], 8 g urea was dissolved in 80 mL DI water and 8 g melamine was added. After stirring well, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was placed in a thermostat, kept at 180 °C for 24 h, and then taken out. After the reaction solution was naturally cooled to room temperature, it was filtered. The precursor product was washed with DI water and ethanol for several times, respectively, and then dried in a vacuum drying box at 60 °C overnight. The white precursor was gently ground for 5 min, then put into a graphite boat and placed in a tube furnace, heated to 550 °C at a heating rate of 2.3 °C/min, kept for 4 h, and naturally cooled to room temperature, thus obtaining the light-yellow TCN sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQDs solution and powder
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous literature [27], 15 g of citric acid and 5 g of urea were dissolved in 75 mL of DI water and stirred for half an hour. The solution was put into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was placed in a thermostat and kept at 180 °C for 5 h and then taken out. After the reaction solution was naturally cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged at 4000 r/min for 20 min to obtain reddish-brown N-CQDs solution, which was stored at 4 °C for later use. The N-CQDs powder was obtained after the N-CQDs solution was dried in a vacuum drying oven at a temperature of 60 °C overnight.

TCN (0.2 g) was dispersed in the ethanol/water solution (VH2O:VCH3OH = 1:1), then, 2 mL of N-CQDs solution was added and stirred for 30 min. During the stirring process, a portion of N-CQDs were adsorbed on the surface of TCN. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was placed in a thermostat and held at 160 °C for 12 h and then taken out. The final brown solid was named 2-NCQDs/TCN.

TCN (0.2 g) was dispersed in the ethanol/water solution (VH2O:VCH3OH = 1:1), subsequently, 0.187 mmol of ZnCl2, 0.373 mmol of InCl3·4H2O and 1.5 mmol of TAA (the mass ratio of Zn: In: S (MZn: MIn: MS) = 1:2:8) were added to the above mixed solution, and then, 2 mL of N-CQDs solution was added and stirred for 30 min. During the stirring process, a portion of N-CQDs were adsorbed on the surface of TCN. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was placed in a thermostat and held at 160 °C for 12 h and then taken out. During this process, ZnIn2S4 was grown on the surface of N-CQDs/TCN and a portion of N-CQDs were also loaded on the surface of ZnIn2S4. After the reaction solution was naturally cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged and the product was washed several times with water and ethanol, and dried in a vacuum drying oven at a temperature of 60 °C overnight to obtain reddish-brown sample (2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN). Depending on the amount of N-CQDs solution added (1 mL and 3 mL), 1-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN and 3-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN were obtained.

The preparation process of ZnIn2S4/TCN samples (ZIS/TCN) was basically the same as described above, except that N-CQDs solution was not added. The performance of ZIS/TCN was affected by the load amount of ZnIn2S4.

Silica sol was used as a binder between the photocatalyst and Ti electrode substrate. The advantages and preparation process of silica sol can refer to the supplementary material Text S1 and S2, respectively. 0.05 g of composite photocatalyst was put into the mixed solution of 2 mL ethanol and 500 μL silica sol. After ultrasonic stirring for 5 min, the sample was loaded on the surface of Ti sheet using drop-coating method, and then the Ti sheet was dried at 100 °C for 5 min. The drop-coating and drying steps were repeated 4 times, and then the electrode was calcined in a tube furnace at 140 °C for 2 h to obtain N-CQDs/ZnIn2S4/TCN/Ti electrode. The optimum calcination temperature and calcination time were 140 °C and 2 h respectively. The optimization results of calcination temperature and calcination time are shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3 respectively in the Supplementary material. According to the different photocatalysts (1-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN, 2-N-CQDs/ZnIn2S4/TCN and 3-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN) added, the photoanodes were labeled 1-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti, 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti and 3-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti, respectively. The flow chart of the preparation of the electrode is shown in Scheme 1 . TCN/Ti, ZnIn2S4/Ti (ZIS/Ti), 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti and ZnIn2S4/TCN/Ti (40-ZIS/TCN/Ti) photoanodes were prepared using the same preparation steps as NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanodes, but TCN, ZIS, NCQDs/TCN and 40-ZIS/TCN photocatalysts were respectively added instead of NCQDs/ZIS/TCN photocatalyst.

The preparation of CuxO/Cu cathode can refer to our previous work [28]. In brief, the pre-treated Cu sheet negative electrode and carbon rod positive electrode were respectively connected negative and positive terminal of a DC power and immersed in the solution containing 3 mM Cu(NO3)2, 0.1 M NH4Cl and 0.05 M KCl, and maintained at 70 °C for 2 h with a continuous current of 30 mA. According to the results of characterization of cathode (Fig. S6 and Text S3), both Cu2O and CuO existed on the surface of Cu, so the cathode was expressed as CuxO/Cu.

The constructed photoanode and CuxO/Cu cathode were used in a two-electrode setup to test the organic contaminants deterioration and power production capabilities of PFC. The photoanode and CuxO/Cu cathode were placed parallel to each other at a 3.0 cm distance in the rectangular quartz reactor (5 cm on each side) with a peristaltic pump to circulate the reaction solution, and connected with a copper wire in the experiment on organic contaminants deterioration, or connected respectively with negative and positive terminal of the electrochemical workstation in the experiment on electricity generation performance. 125 mL of 10 mg·L−1 RhB solution was added into the reactor. A 500 W xenon lamp, whose ultraviolet light below 420 nm was filtered using a filter, and light intensity was determined to be 50.0 mW·cm−2 (ST-500, China), was installed about 25 cm away on the photocatalyst side of the photoanode. After starting the peristaltic pump, the reaction timer was stared while the xenon lamp was turned on. Every ten min, the absorbance of 3 mL sample of RhB solution was measured at its maximum absorption wavelength of 554 nm with a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The degradation rates of TC (20 mg·L−1) and BPA (10 mg·L−1) were determined by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). For the HPLC detection of TC, the mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 0.01 mol/L oxalic acid (V/V% = 27.5:72.5), the wavelength of ultraviolet detector was set at 357 nm, the flow rate was 1 mL/min, the injection volume was 20 μL, and the column temperature was 30 °C. Under these conditions, the retention time of TC was 3.627 min. For the HPLC detection of BPA, the mobile phase was a mixture of methanol and acetic acid (V/V% = 65:35), the wavelength of ultraviolet detector was 275 nm, the flow rate was 1 mL/min, the sample volume was 10 μL, and the column temperature was 35 °C. Under these conditions, the retention time of BPA was 5.386 min. The concentration of H2O2 was determined spectrophotometrically using potassium titanium oxalate at the maximum absorption wavelength of 385 nm. Unless otherwise stated, the experiment was conducted under the optimal conditions of initial pH of 3.0, circulating flow rate of 190 mL·min−1, and electrolyte of 0.1 M Na2SO4. To make this paper more concise, the optimization results of the above optimal conditions are not listed. Each experiment was conducted three times to confirm the credibility and reproducibility of the data.

The crystalline phase of the photocatalyst was investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD, XRD-7000S, Shimadzu). The organic components of photoanode were identified using Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS 50, America). The morphology and microstructure of the photoanode were examined with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU-8010, Shimadzu). UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was used to assess the light absorption spectrum of the photoanode (DRS, UV2600, Japan). With the use of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, USA), the elements and chemical valence states of the photoanode were determined. The efficiency of photoanode and PFC was evaluated using an electrochemical workstation (CHI650E, Beijing Huake Putian Technology Co., Ltd.). The characteristic curves of the photocurrent response and the linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) was studied with a scanning rate of 10 mV/s, using a three-electrode system with platinum as the counter electrode, calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and the prepared photoanode as the working electrode. The electrochemical impedance test (EIS) and Mott Schottky test (MS) used the same three-electrode system, except that the electrolyte was 1 M NaCl and 0.1 M Na2SO4, respectively. An UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV6100, Shanghai Metash Analysis Instrument Co., Ltd.) was used to acquire the absorbance analysis of RhB and H2O2. Concentrations of TC and BPA were determined by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Shimadzu LC-20 AD) with C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm). The concentration of total organic carbon (TOC) in the solution was measured using a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-L, SHIMADZU).

In order to study the microscopic morphology of electrode material, SEM analysis was carried out. The SEM images of TCN/Ti and ZIS/Ti electrodes demonstrated that tubular g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 were loaded on Ti sheet in the form of tubular and flower balls, respectively (Fig. 1a and b). The SEM image of 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti (Fig. 1c) showed that the surface of tubular g-C3N4 became rougher than that of pure TCN electrode, which proved that ZnIn2S4 was successfully deposited on the surface of tubular g-C3N4. The SEM image of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti (Fig. 1d and e) also demonstrated that ZnIn2S4 was successfully loaded on the TCN surface, but the loading of N-CQDs could not be detected clearly. Therefore, it is necessary to observe the surface morphology of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti by TEM and HTEM.

The TEM and HTEM patterns of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti are shown in Fig. 2a, b and c. The tubular material was clearly displayed in the field of view (Fig. 2a). The enlarged figure (Fig. 2b) of the selected white circular area (Fig. 2a) showed that N-CQDs were loaded on the tubular g-C3N4 and ZnIn2S4 composites in an irregular microsphere shape, and their particle size was about 7–16 nm, this was consistent with the findings of previous [27]. HTEM pattern (Fig. 2c) accurately showed a planar lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was in line with the (101) lattice plane of graphitic carbon, but according to previous studies, carbon nitride was amorphous diffraction and had no lattice fringes [22]. As a result, this one belonged to the lattice fringes of N-CQDs. This proved that N-CQDs had been successfully loaded. The 0.25 nm lattice fringes equivalent to (110) planes of ZnIn2S4 were located around N-CQDs. This further proved the successful preparation of ternary 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti composite.

The elemental mapping images of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti showed that Zn, In, S, C, and N elements were uniformly distributed on its surface (Fig. 2d and e). This further demonstrated the construction of NCQDs/ZIS/TCN heterostructure with close interface contact, which was in line with the results of SEM. This close contact was beneficial to the formation of an electron transfer region on the interface [29].

In order to further determine the material composition and crystal type of the composite electrode material, XRD experiments were carried out in this study. As shown in Fig. 3a, in the XRD plot of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti composite electrode material, (006), (102), (110), and (1012) planes represented the characteristic peaks of ZIS (JCPDS No. 72–0773). Compared with the XRD plot of pure ZIS, the (104) peak of the composite was not obvious, which might be due to the decrease of ZIS content resulted from the doping of N-CQDs, thus weakening the peak. In addition, the diffraction peak at 27.8° for (102) plane was significantly wider and gradually shifted to a higher degree. This originated from the substitution of S by C and N in the doped quantum dots. Because the ionic radii of C and N were smaller, the crystal radius and d-spacing were reduced [30]. The peak of the complex located at 10.9° corresponded to the ordered arrangement of the heptazine unit (100) within the TCN layer. Compared with the XRD pattern of pure TCN, the diffraction peak of (100) lattice plane shifted from 13.5° to 10.9°, this phenomenon might be attributed to the intralayer arrangement of the N-lined heterocyclic ring during a one-step solvothermal process [31]. Moreover, the characteristic peak of pure TCN at 27.4°, equivalent to the interlayer stacking of the crystalline surface of the heptazine structure (002), was masked by the strong peak of ZIS at (102) in the XRD patterns of ZIS loaded composites. In contrast, due to the absence of ZIS loading, the characteristic peak of 2-NCQDs/TCN still appeared at 27.4° [32]. Compared to 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti, some of the weak peaks, such as (006) and (1012) became inconspicuous in the XRD pattern of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti, which might be due to the loading of N-CQDs with high crystallinity. The XRD results showed that the ternary photoanode material was successfully composite, which was in agreement with the previous characterization findings.

To further confirm the effect of silica sol drip coating on the composition of the material, the XRD pattern of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN sample was also recorded. The results showed that, compared with the XRD pattern of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti material, there was no SiO2 heterogeneous peak, indicating that silica sol drip coating did not affect the composition of the electrode material. This might be due to the fact that the silica sol content in the electrode was very small (only 500 μL).

In order to explore whether TCN was successfully loaded into the composite 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN, FT-IR spectra of ZIS, N-CQDs, TCN, 2-NCQDs/TCN and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3, the peaks of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN, 2-NCQDs/TCN and pure TCN were basically the same, indicating that TCN had been successfully loaded into 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN. In the spectrum of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN, the absorption band at 810 cm−1 represented the breathing model of the tri-s-triazine unit [33], the absorption band group at 1080–1680 cm−1 represented the N-C=N heterocyclic tensile vibration [34], the absorption peaks at 1243, 1326 and 1407 cm−1 represented the tensile vibration of C-NH-C, and the absorption peaks at 1567 and 1637 cm−1 represented the tensile vibration of CN [35–37]. Besides, the absorption band at 3000–3300 cm−1 corresponded to NH and leftover amino groups that were adsorbed on the TCN [38–41]. The absorption peak of TCN, 2-NCQDs/TCN and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN at 2372 cm−1 represented the CO2 adsorbed by tubular g-C3N4 [42]. The characteristic peaks of N-CQDs appeared at 1576.7 cm−1 and 842.7 cm−1, which were ascribed to tensile vibration of CN and breathing vibration of triazine, respectively. In addition, obvious characteristic peak was not detected in the FT-IR spectrum of ZIS, which was basically in line with the finding of previous study [31]. In conclusion, FT-IR results showed that TCN had been successfully loaded into the composite 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN, which agreed with the XRD findings.

In order to study the elemental composition of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN material, XPS analysis was performed. The survey spectrum of XPS showed that the composite material contained five elements: N, C, S, Zn (Fig. 5a), which was in agreement with the results of TEM-EDS (Fig. 2d and e).

As shown in Fig. 5b, the C1s spectrum of TCN exhibited peaks at 288.9, 288.2, 286.4 and 284.8 eV. These peaks were attributed to carbon bonded to oxygen (C-O/C=O), sp2-hybridized carbon of heptazine units (N-C=N), terminal amino groups-bonded carbon (C-NHX), and standard carbon (C-C/C=C), respectively [43]. In the XPS spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 5c), three peaks could be observed. The peaks at 399.9 and 398.8 eV represented NC3 and C=N-C, respectively, while the peak at 401.2 eV belonged to N-HX [44]. In Fig. 5d, the peaks of S2p at 163.1 and 161.3 eV belonged to S2p1/2 and S2p3/2, respectively. In Fig. 5e, the peaks of In3d at 453.0 and 445.1 eV belonged to In3d3/2 and In3d5/2, respectively, which both belonged to In3+. In Fig. 5f, the peaks of Zn2p at 1045.9 and 1022.3 eV, representing Zn2p1/2 and Zn2p3/2, respectively, demonstrated the presence of Zn2+ [45,46]. The results of XPS further showed that the ternary composite electrode was successfully prepared.

In order to investigate the light absorption capacity of individual photoanode materials, DRS experiments were done in this study. The absorption band edges of TCN/Ti, ZIS/Ti, 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti were 464 nm, 585 nm, 519 nm and 532 nm, respectively (Fig. 6a). This indicated that all electrode materials were visible light responsive. Compared with TCN/Ti and ZIS/Ti, the absorption band edge of 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti shifted, suggesting the interaction between TCN and ZIS. Compared with the binary photoanode (40-ZIS/TCN/Ti), the absorption band edge of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti had a red-shift and stronger light absorption in the visible and infrared light regions, indicating that the doping of N-CQDs not only expanded the light absorption range, but also effectively enhanced the intensity of light absorption. The energy band gap (E
g ) of TCN/Ti, ZIS/Ti, 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti was obtained by Eq. 1 and Tauc's plots (Fig. 6b) [47].
(1)

hν
= A
hν −
E g
n / 2
where α, υ, h and A represent Planck's constant, light frequency, absorption coefficient and constant, respectively, and n is a constant depending on the optical transition properties of the prepared semiconductor.

According to previous literature [48], the n value of both TCN and ZIS is 4, so the E
g of TCN/Ti, ZIS/Ti, 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti is 2.89, 2.46, 2.83 and 2.77 eV, respectively. In comparison with TCN/Ti and ZIS/Ti electrodes, the band gap width of 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti lied between them, further suggesting the formation of a heterojunction between TCN and ZIS. The binary electrode material not only enhanced the light absorption range of TCN, but also overcame the drawback of ZIS that was prone to photo-corrosion, indicating that the combination of TCN and ZIS could make up for each other's shortcomings. Compared with 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti electrode, the narrow bandgap of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti further suggested that the doping of N-CQDs led to the interaction between N-CQDs with ZIS and TCN and the formation of Schottky junction, which greatly broadened photo-response range of the electrode material. This is consistent with the findings of DRS analysis.

In order to study the photoelectric properties of photoanode materials, LSV analysis test was conducted. After optimization, when the mass ratio of ZnIn2S4 was 40 wt%, the performance of ZIS/TCN was the best. The detailed optimization results can refer to Fig. S1 in Supplementary material. As shown in Fig. 7a, the photocurrent densities of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti, 1-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti, 3-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti, 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti, 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti, TCN/Ti and ZIS/Ti were 11.19, 8.59, 7.56, 5.55, 2.92, 2.08 and 1.89 mA/cm2, respectively. The 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanode had the highest photocurrent density, which indicated that it had better photoelectric performance [49].

In order to further investigate the photoelectric properties of different photoanodes, the photoelectric conversion efficiency was calculated by Eq. 2 [50]. Among the photoanodes, 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti achieved the highest photoelectric conversion efficiency (0.098 %), which was 1.30, 1.44, 1.94, 4.08, 5.02 and 5.76 times that of 1-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti (0.076 %), 3-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti (0.068 %), 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti (0.051 %), 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti (0.024 %), TCN/Ti (0.020 %) and ZIS/Ti (0.017 %), respectively. The results were in line with the results of LSV analysis. This proved that the doping of N-CQDs acted as photosensitizer to further enhance the absorption and utilization of light energy, and effectively improved the photoelectric conversion efficiency of photocatalyst, which was consistent with the assumption in introduction and the data of DRS.
(2)
η =
I
1.23 − U
P
× 100 %
where P, U, I and η are the optical power density (50.0 mW·cm−2), applied potential vs. Ag/AgCl, photocurrent density and photoelectric conversion efficiency, respectively.

In addition, the electrochemical impedance (EIS) test was carried out for TCN/Ti, ZIS/Ti, 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti, 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanodes. The inset shows the equivalent circuit for fitting Nyquist plots, where the Rs, Rct and CPE refer to electrolyte resistance, the charge transfer resistance and constant phase element, respectively. The smaller the radius of the semicircle of EIS curve, the smaller the charge transfer resistance of the electrode [51]. As shown in Fig. 6b, the internal resistance of different photoanodes was ZIS/Ti > TCN/Ti > 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti > 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti > 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti. This indicated that the formation of heterojunction could significantly reduce the internal resistance of the electrode, which might be because the constructed heterojunction could provide a channel for electron transfer and effectively promote the separation of photogenerated carriers. In addition, this also indicated that the addition of N-CQDs could significantly reduce the internal resistance of the electrode and make the electrode have better photoelectric performance. This might be due to the fact that N-CQDs could not only act as an electron transport medium between different components to accelerate the electron transfer, but also provide more channels for electron transfer between heterojunctions. This was consistent with the assumption in the introduction that N-CQDs could promote the transport of TCN/ZIS S-type heterojunction to make full use of the heterojunction properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RhB degradation and electricity generation performance of different PFCs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the performance of PFC systems, six PFC systems composed of different photoanodes and CuxO/Cu cathode were established, which were called 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu, 1-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu, 3-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu, 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu, 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu, TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu and ZIS/Ti-CuxO/Cu. Besides, RhB degradation rates of adsorption (2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti and CuxO/Cu unconnected in dark), photolysis (only irradiation without 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanode and CuxO/Cu cathode), and primary cell (2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti and CuxO/Cu connected in dark) processes were investigated.

As shown in Fig. 8a, RhB degradation rates of photolysis and adsorption were 2.22 % and 5.70 %, respectively, indicating that both photolysis and adsorption had only a minor effect on the degradation of RhB. Proper adsorption of RhB was beneficial to its photocatalytic degradation. RhB degradation rate of primary cell was 17.18 % ± 1.10 %, suggesting that there was a difference of Fermi energy level between the photoanode and cathode materials, which promoted the degradation of RhB. The degradation rate of 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (71.56 % ± 0.94 %) and 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (64.65 % ± 1.47 %) was higher than that of TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (54.45 % ± 1.11 %) and ZIS/Ti-CuxO/Cu (50.78 % ± 1.78 %), which proved that the formation of heterojunction effectively increased the electron-hole separation rate, thereby increasing the degradation rate by accelerating the production of active substances. The degradation rates of all the three NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu PFCs were higher than those of the PFCs with binary composite and monadic material photoanodes. This was because N-CQDs could be used not only as photosensitizers to improve light absorption, but also as electron transfer medium and electronic warehouse to promote the separation of electron-hole pairs, thereby boosting the battery performance. The degradation efficiency of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (95.89 % ± 0.69 %) was greater than that of 1-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (91.71 % ± 0.83 %) and 3-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (87.76 % ± 0.75 %). This might be due to the fact that too little doping of N-CQDs could not effectively promote electron transfer, while excessive doping of N-CQDs not only made N-CQDs aggregate with each other, but also affected the light absorption of TCN and ZIS because of the strong absorbance of N-CQDs to visible light, and thus reducing the degradation performance. The pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Eq. 3) was used to fit the degradation data of RhB. Only the degradation data of RhB in the first 15 min were selected for fitting, because the intermediate products produced in the process of RhB degradation and the decrease of RhB concentration would affect the degradation of RhB, which would lead to the error of fitting results. The results (Fig. 8b) showed that 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu had the highest degradation rate constant (k = 0.085 min−1), which was 1.21, 1.42, 1.77, 1.98, 2.83 and 3.15 times that of 1-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (k = 0.070 min−1), 3-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (k = 0.060 min−1), 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (k = 0.048 min−1), 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (k = 0.043 min−1), TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (k = 0.030 min−1) and ZIS/Ti-CuxO/Cu (k = 0.027 min−1), respectively.
(3)
− ln
C t
C 0
= kt
where k (min−1), t (min), C0 and Ct (mg·L−1) represent the pseudo-first-order reaction rate constant, the reaction time, initial concentration and the concentration of RhB solution at time t, respectively.

Moreover, the power generation performance of different PFC systems was compared and tested. As shown in Fig. 8c, 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu had the best power generation performance, which was in line with the results of LSV, EIS and RhB degradation performance, indicating that the superior photoelectric performance was mainly attributed to the properties of the photoanode. The maximum photocurrent density and maximum power density of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu were 0.54 mA·cm−2 and 71.10 μW·cm−2, respectively, which were higher than those of 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (0.40 mA·cm−2 and 41.46 μW·cm−2), 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (0.30 mA·cm−2 and 23.47 μW·cm−2), TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (0.21 mA·cm−2 and 15.08 μW·cm−2) and ZIS/Ti-CuxO/Cu (0.20 mA·cm−2 and 9.45 μW·cm−2). This might be attributable to the fact that the doping of N-CQDs greatly improved the electron transfer rate and light absorption capacity. These findings indicated that the strategy of improving the performance of PFC system by heterojunction construction and N-CQDs doping was feasible. At the same time, in order to verify the magnitude of the photocurrent, a current-time experiment was also done in this study. As shown in Fig. 8d, the ternary photoanode PFC system had good visible light photoelectric response [52]. Its maximum photocurrent intensity soared to 0.5 mA·cm−2 when the lamp was turned on, and dropped to 0.05 mA·cm−2 when the lamp was turned off. The maximum photocurrent intensity of the ternary photoanode PFC system was higher than that of 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (0.36 mA·cm−2), 2-NCQDs/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (0.25 mA·cm−2), TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu (0.15 mA·cm−2) and ZIS/Ti-CuxO/Cu (0.13 mA·cm−2). This was due to the large figure of merit of N-CQDs and the sp2 of carbon atoms in N-CQDs provided extra orbitals for photogenerated e− [53–56], which made it tend to plunder electrons from other components, thereby becoming an electron storehouse promoting the separation of electron-hole pairs. This corresponded to the data from the EIS. It was worth noting that when there was no illumination, the photocurrent did not disappear completely, but remained at 0.05 mA·cm−2, which might be due to the difference of Fermi level between the photoanode and cathode [57].

In addition, the changes in the absorption spectrum of RhB during the degradation were also studied. The peak intensity of RhB at 554 nm became smaller and smaller with the increase of reaction time (Fig. 8e), indicating that RhB was gradually degraded. The wavelength of the peak was blue-shifted with time, indicating that RhB was degraded using the deethyl process [58].

Initial pH can significantly affect the degradation efficiency of RhB. The results of the effect of initial pH are shown in Fig. S4. The results showed that the degradation efficiency gradually decreased with the increase of the initial pH, and it was the highest when the pH was 3.0. This is because OH− ions can inhibit the production of active substances such as •OH [59]. The higher the initial pH, the stronger the inhibition and the lower the degradation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other organic pollutants degradation and electricity generation performance of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN-CuxO/Cu PFC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the performance of the 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu PFC system in more depth, the maximum power density, degradation rate and TOC removal rate of the system were investigated with 20 mg·L−1 of TC and 10 mg·L−1 of BPA as the target pollutants. Experimental results showed (Fig. 9a) that the degradation rate of RhB (95.89 %) was higher than that of TC (83.44 %) and BPA (50.66 %) within 30 min. The maximum power density of RhB (71.10 μW·cm−2) was higher than that of TC (59.35 μW·cm−2) and BPA (37.70 μW·cm−2) (Fig. 9b), and the TOC removal rate of RhB (33.65 % ± 2.17 %) was also higher than that of TC (21.71 % ± 1.17 %) and BPA (12.33 % ± 1.65 %) (Fig. 9c). The TOC removal rate confirmed that the system mineralized organic wastewater to a certain extent. However, the removal rate of TOC was far lower than the degradation rate, indicating that most of RhB, TC and BPA were only degraded into small molecular organic compounds and not completely mineralized into CO2 and H2O. The best PFC performance with RhB target pollutant might be attributed to the different molecular structure of TC, RhB and BPA (Fig. S5), as well as the dye sensitization of RhB [60]. As shown in Fig. S5, although the molecular structures of RhB, TC and BPA all contain benzene rings. However, RhB molecule has CN double bond, which is easy to break and the degradation of RhB is realized by deethylation, which can be further confirmed by the results of UV–vis absorption spectrum change in Fig. 8e. BPA molecule has a very symmetrical molecular structure and strong chemical stability, which makes it difficult to be degraded. The stability of TC molecular structure is between RhB and BPA. Therefore, the order of degradation efficiency of RhB, TC and BPA was RhB > TC > BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and visible-light response of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu PFC

ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the working mechanism of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system, a series of mechanistic experiments was conducted.

First, in order to explore the effect of CuxO/Cu cathode on the performance of the PFC, the degradation experiment was performed with Pt cathode instead of CuxO/Cu cathode. As shown in Fig. 11a, the degradation efficiency of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-Pt system was not much different from that of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system, indicating that CuxO/Cu had little influence on the performance of PFC, and its excellent performance was mainly originated from the action of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanode. The difference between the above two PFC systems might be because of a difference in Fermi energy levels between CuxO/Cu and Pt cathodes and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanode [58]. In general, electron charge flow is closely related to the work function, and the electron transfer process from photoanode to cathode is strongly influenced by the alignment of their work functions [65]. Because the Fermi level between the photoanode and CuxO/Cu cathode was different, the photoelectrons tended to transfer from the photoanode with higher Fermi level to the CuxO/Cu cathode with lower Fermi level. In addition, due to the difference of work functions between the two electrodes, a theoretical bias voltage (Vp) as a self-driving force can be generated between two electrodes [66]. These theories indicated that the photogenerated electrons in photoanode can be quickly transferred from the photoanode through external circuit (mediator) by self-driving force, forming PFC mechanism [67].

Second, in order to investigate the active substances involved in the degradation of RhB, the quenching experiments in different systems were done. It is reported that p-BQ (0.12 M), TBA (0.34 M), EDTA-2Na (0.12 M) and FA (0.07 M) can quench •O2 −, •OH, h+ and 1O2 [68,69], respectively. Fig. 11b shows the experimental results. The degradation rate of ZIS/Ti-CuxO/Cu system decreased by 28.22 %, 4.38 %, 14.85 % and 6.37 % after the addition of p-BQ, TBA, EDTA-2Na and FA, respectively, indicating that h+ and •O2 − were the main active substances involved in RhB degradation, while 1O2 was also involved in the reaction to a certain extent. The degradation rate of ZIS/Ti-CuxO/Cu system did not decrease significantly after adding TBA, which might be due to the fact that the oxidation potential of ZIS did not reach the condition of •OH generation. For the TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system, the degradation rate decreased by 23.66 %, 19.19 %, 15.85 % and 6.34 %, respectively, indicating that •OH, •O2 − and h+ were the main substances involved in the degradation of RhB, while 1O2 was involved in the reaction to some extent. For the 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system, the degradation rate decreased by 20.92 %, 25.61 %, 36.32 %, and 9.63 %, respectively. Compared with the single photocatalyst photoanode system, the h+ and •OH produced by 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system increased, which suggested that a heterojunction was constructed between TCN and ZIS, which enhanced the action and oxidation ability of h+, resulting in the increase of the production of •OH. So, for the 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system, •O2 −, •OH, h+, and 1O2 were involved in the degradation reaction. For the 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system, the degradation rate decreased by 30.28 %, 48.95 %, 35.55 %, and 9.89 %, respectively, which suggested that •O2 −, •OH, h+, and 1O2 were involved in the degradation reaction. The effect of •OH on degradation in 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system was significantly elevated compared to 40-ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system. To further clarify the reaction mechanism, H2O2 produced in the degradation process of different systems was determined by potassium titanium oxalate spectrophotometry. After 30 min of reaction, the concentration of H2O2 in 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system was 0.4 mg·L−1, but H2O2 was not detected in TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu, ZIS/Ti-CuxO/Cu and ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu systems. H2O2 could not only directly react with RhB, but also obtain e− (Eq. 15), and react with •O2 − to generate •OH (Eq. 16), thus improving the degradation effect. The above results suggested that N-CQDs promoted the production of H2O2 and •OH in 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system, thus enhancing its degradation performance.

Third, in order to investigate the energy band position and size of different electrode materials, Mott-Schottky curves were drawn through impedance-potential experiments. From the Mott Schottky curves (Fig. 12a-c), it can be seen that all four electrode materials, TCN, ZIS, 40-ZIS/TCN and 2-NCDQDs/ZIS/TCN, are n-type semiconductors. Their flat band potentials were −0.81, −1.05, −1.02 and −0.98 eV, respectively. It is widely known that the conduction potential (E
CB ) of n-type semiconductor is generally considered to be 0.2 eV more positive than the flat band potential (E
fb ) [70]. Therefore, the E
CB of TCN, ZIS, 40-ZIS/TCN and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN were −0.61, −0.85, −0.82, and −0.78 eV, respectively. According to the DRS data (Fig. 6b), the E
g of TCN, ZIS, 40-ZIS/TCN, and 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN were 2.89, 2.47, 2.83, and 2.77 eV, respectively. Therefore, according to Eq. 4, E
CB and valence-band potential (E
VB ) of TCN were −0.61 and 2.28 eV, respectively. ECB and EVB of ZIS were −0.85 and 1.61 eV, respectively. Because the EVB of ZIS (1.61 eV) was lower than that of H2O/•OH (2.20 eV) [60], ZIS could not efficiently generate •OH, which was in agreement with the findings of quenching experiments. ECB and EVB were −0.82 and 2.01 eV for 40-ZIS/TCN and −0.78 and 1.99 eV for 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN, respectively.
(4)
E VB
=
E g
+
E CB

According to the experimental results, the E
fb of 40-ZIS/TCN was −1.02 eV, which was different from that of ZIS (−1.05 eV) and TCN (−0.81 eV). The E
fb position of ZIS (−1.05 eV) was much higher than that of TCN (−0.81 eV), suggesting that the Fermi level of ZIS was higher than that of TCN, which led to trend of electron transfer from ZIS to TCN until the Fermi levels of ZIS and TCN were the same. The E
fb position (−1.02 eV) of 40-ZIS/TCN was located between TCN and ZIS, and an internal electric field was formed between them. After both ZIS and TCN produced photogenerated electrons and holes under visible light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons produced by TCN were transferred to ZIS under the action of the built-in electric field [71]. Moreover, the E
fb position of 40-ZIS/TCN was located near that of ZIS, from which could be concluded that the CB position of 40-ZIS/TCN was located near that of ZIS. When it was irradiated by visible light, the photogenerated electrons would aggregate at the CB of ZIS in the mixture. If a traditional type II heterojunction exists between ZIS and TCN, the photogenerated electrons of ZIS will be transferred to the CB of TCN, so in this case, the photogenerated electrons will be accumulated on the CB of TCN, which is inconsistent with the previous conclusion, that is, the photogenerated electrons will be accumulated on the CB of ZIS. In addition, the photogenerated electrons cannot be transferred from ZIS to TCN under the action of the built-in electric field. So traditional type II heterojunction cannot explain the electron transfer between ZIS and TCN (Scheme 2a). If there is an S-type heterojunction between ZIS and TCN, then under visible light irradiation, TCN and ZIS are excited by visible light to produce electrons and holes, and under the action of the internal electric field, the electrons on the CB of TCN are transferred to the VB of ZIS and annihilated by combining with the holes of ZIS, which leads to the aggregation of photogenerated holes and electrons on the VB of TCN and the CB of ZIS, respectively. The VB position of the complex was located at 1.99 eV, which was different from the original VB position of TCN (2.28 eV), which might be due to the change of band gap position and size due to the interaction between the components after contact [72], so the S-type heterojunction was agreement with the experimental results, and the production of S-type heterojunction greatly promoted the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and improved the photocatalytic activity of the system (Scheme. 2b).

For 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN material, due to the high work function of N-CQDs and the low Fermi level, the incorporation of N-CQDs may produce Schottky junction with 40-ZIS/TCN, which will continuously harvest electrons from ZIS and TCN [50]. The E
fb position of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN (−0.98 eV) was lower than that of 40-ZIS/TCN (−1.02 eV), from which could be concluded that the Fermi energy level of 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN decreased after 40-ZIS/TCN was doped with N-CQDs, which proved the existence of Schottky junction between N-CQDs and 40-ZIS/TCN. When N-CQDs came into contact with ZIS and TCN, a built-in electric field was generated, so that electrons flowed into N-CQDs continuously until the Fermi levels of N-CQDs, ZIS and TCN were the same. Once the electrons of N-CQDs were consumed by reaction, under the action of Schottky junction, the electrons of ZIS and TCN were continuously transferred to N-CQDs. Therefore, the primary cell system degraded 17.18 % ± 1.10 % of RhB in 30 min (Fig. 8a). This was because that the electrons on N-CQDs reacted with O2 through the difference in Fermi energy levels between the cathode and the anode to generate •O2 − to degrade RhB [73]. In summary, the S-type heterojunction between ZIS and TCN under light irradiation provided the necessary guarantee for the generation of photogenerated electrons, the N-CQDs acted like an electron storehouse to efficiently separate and apply e−. Thus, it can be understood that the combination of S-type heterojunction and Schottky junction is very promising and significant for the development of photoanode materials (Scheme 2c).

Based on the data and analysis presented above, the working mechanism of the 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti-CuxO/Cu system was proposed. First, under visible light irradiation, photogenerated electrons and holes were produced by both ZIS and TCN (Eq. 5), and N-CQDs as sensitized materials greatly increased the absorption and utilization of visible and infrared light by the 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanode (Fig. 6a). Under the action of the S-type heterojunction, the photogenerated electrons of TCN combined with the photogenerated holes of ZIS (Eq. 6), which led to the annihilation of the electrons in the CB of TCN and the holes in the VB of ZIS, and the aggregation of photogenerated electrons in the CB of ZIS and holes in the VB of TCN, and the N-CQDs also acted as electron transfer mediators to make the electron transfer more uniform in the S-type heterojunction. Since the oxidation potential of h+ in VB of TCN (1.99 eV) was more negative than that of H2O/•OH (2.2 eV), h+ could not react with H2O at the surface of photoanode to produce •OH (Eq. 7). However, it could not only react with OH− to produce •OH (Eq. 8) to degrade pollutant, but also directly participate in in the degradation of pollutant (Eq. 9). In addition, due to the Schottky junction between N-CQDs and ZIS/TCN, the photogenerated electrons of TCN and ZIS flowed to the N-CQDs in a steady stream (Eq. 10–11), and the sp2 carbon atoms of the N-CQDs provided orbitals for the transfer of e−. Thus, the e− aggregated on ZIS and N-CQDs was transferred to the cathode due to the difference in Fermi energy level between the anode and the cathode, so that the directional transfer of the e− created an electric current. The e− aggregated on ZIS and N-CQDs could also react with DO in the solution to produce •O2 − because the potential of ZIS and N-CQDs (−0.78 eV) was much higher than that of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV) [60] (Eq. 12). Also, DO in the solution could react with protons and e− to produce H2O (Eq. 13). Because of the strong electron delocalization ability of the Schottky junction, •O2 − could react with excess e− to produce H2O2 (Eq. 14). The presence of H2O2 could not only continue to react with e− to generate •OH, but also react with •O2 − to generate •OH and O2 (Eq. 15–16), besides, •O2 − could also be converted to H2O by reaction with •OH (Eq. 17). In addition, •O2 − could react with •OH or h+ to form 1O2 (Eq. 18–19), so •OH, •O2 − and 1O2 were all involved in the degradation of pollutants (Eq. 20). In this way, a visible light response PFC system combining organic pollutant treatment and power production was successfully constructed (Scheme 3 ).

In conclusion, in this work, 2-NCQDs/ZIS/TCN/Ti photoanode was successfully prepared by a simple one-pot solvothermal method and silica sol drop-coating method, and employed in the PFC system with CuxO/Cu cathode. The PFC system possessed not only the ability to efficiently degrade different organic pollutants in a short period of time, but also strong electrical properties, which were achieved by the combination of the strong photogenerated electron output of ZIS/TCN S-type heterojunction and the excellent photogenerated electron separation rate of N-CQDs/TCN and N-CQDs/ZIS Schottky junctions. N-CQDs was used as sensitizing material to enhance the absorption and utilization of visible and infrared light, as electron warehouses to enhance the separation of photogenerated carriers, and as an electron transfer medium between different components to ensure the electron transfer between the components more uniform. •OH, h+, •O2 − and 1O2 were involved in the degradation of RhB, and N-CQDs doping accelerated the production of H2O2 and •OH, thus enhancing the degradation effect. The power generation capacity, photoelectric conversion efficiency and RhB degradation rate of the PFC were 71.10 μW·cm−2, 0.098 % and 95.89 %, respectively. It is believed that this work will provide new direction and idea for the preparation and application of high efficiency photoanode.
